sound_type: bark call + wing-clap display -- sharp 'BARK! BARK!' (500-1500 Hz) combined with rapid wing-clapping beneath the body during aerial display flight; the most multi-modal display in the engine (vocal + mechanical + visual)
ecosystem: Open fields, grasslands, prairies, marshes, agricultural land. Winter visitor in western PNW. Ground nester. Hunts in daylight. Declining due to grassland loss.
season: Winter visitor in western WA (October-April). Year-round east of Cascades. Display flight peaks February-May. Diurnal and crepuscular hunting year-round.

coordinates.description: Short-eared Owls winter in open fields and marshes around Puget Sound lowlands. Breeding primarily east of Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: First Short-eared Owl entry. Sixth consecutive Strigidae. Second Asio species -- completes the Long-eared/Short-eared comparison pair (139-140). Diurnal hunter -- the most diurnal owl in the engine. Wing-clap display is unique. Ground nester. Declining populations. E=5 reflects bark + wing-clap combined display.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: seventh entry total. Six consecutive at 135-140. The most represented family by far.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Asio from Latin 'horned owl.' flammeus from Latin 'flame-colored,' referring to the tawny-orange plumage on the breast and face.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Asio -- second entry (first: Long-eared Owl at 139). Completes the Asio pair.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. Seventh entry. Six consecutive at 135-140.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae continues for sixth consecutive degree. The owl era closes (or continues). Three genera, four species, seven entries.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Bark: 500-1500 Hz. Wing-clap: broadband percussive (no tonal fundamental). Combined: wider acoustic range than any single-mode owl vocalization.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Bark call: 500-1500 Hz. Higher and sharper than Long-eared Owl (300-400 Hz). Percussive quality.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Bark series: 1-3 seconds (2-5 barks). Wing-clap sequence: 2-5 seconds (5-20 claps). Display flight: 30 seconds to several minutes with repeated sequences.
song_metrics.note_structure: BARK + WING-CLAP: Sharp 'BARK! BARK! BARK!' (2-5 barks, rapid) combined with or followed by wing-clapping beneath the body (5-20 claps per sequence). Multi-modal: vocal + mechanical.
song_metrics.note_types: Bark call (sharp, emphatic), wing-clap (percussive, mechanical), raspy scream (during defense), begging call (female/young).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Display sequence: bark series + wing-clap descent. Repeated during display flight. No fixed phrase structure -- sequences vary in length and intensity.
song_metrics.timbre: Bark: sharp, percussive, aggressive. Higher and harder than Long-eared Owl's deep pumping. Wing-clap: clean percussive impulse, like hand-clapping. Combined: a unique dual-texture display.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. The bark + wing-clap combination is louder and more impactful than the Long-eared Owl's pumping (E=4). Carries across open terrain (200-400 meters). The visual display amplifies the acoustic impact.
song_metrics.tempo: Bark: rapid delivery (2-5 per second). Wing-clap: rapid (2-4 per second). Combined: energetic, percussive rhythm during display flight.
song_metrics.context: Courtship display (primary), territorial defense, nest defense. Display flight occurs in daylight. The wing-clap is unique to courtship/territorial display.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The bark + wing-clap display is consistent across the species' circumboreal range.
song_metrics.key_property: BARK + WING-CLAP DISPLAY -- E=5 MULTI-MODAL PERFORMANCE. Vocal bark (500-1500 Hz) + mechanical wing-clap + visual aerial display. The most multi-modal display in the engine. DIURNAL hunter -- the most diurnal owl. Sixth consecutive Strigidae. Second Asio -- completes Long-eared/Short-eared pair (139-140). Opposite to Long-eared in every dimension. Ground nester. Grassland habitat declining. Moth-like wingbeat.

plumage.overall: Tawny-buff and brown streaking. Yellow eyes. Round head with tiny, barely visible ear tufts. Black eye patches create a 'masked' appearance. Long, broad wings with pale patches.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Tiny ear tufts (almost never visible in the field). Black eye patches. Pale wing patches visible in flight. Moth-like buoyant flight over open fields.
plumage.flight_pattern: MOTH-LIKE: buoyant, floppy wingbeat with long wings held in shallow V. Low hunting flight over open fields. Wing-clap display in courtship: climbs high, claps wings beneath body, descends.

physical_description.overall: Length: 34-43 cm. Weight: 200-475 g. Wingspan: 85-110 cm. Medium-sized. Long wings relative to body. Similar size to Long-eared Owl.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Black eye patches, yellow eyes, tiny ear tufts, moth-like flight. Open-country habitat immediately distinguishes from forest owls.
physical_description.field_id: Look for a medium-sized owl flying low over open fields in daylight with a moth-like wingbeat. Black eye patches and buoyant flight are diagnostic. In winter, check open fields, marshes, and agricultural areas near Puget Sound.

nodes.title: Wing-Clap Display -- Aerial Performance
nodes.description: Climbs to 60-100 meters, claps wings beneath body (5-20 claps), descends. Combined with bark calls. The most multi-modal display in the engine: vocal + mechanical + visual. E=5 combined acoustic impact.

nodes.title: Diurnal Hunter -- Daylight Owl
nodes.description: Hunts in daylight with moth-like wingbeat over open fields. Low, quartering flight 1-3 meters above ground. The most diurnal owl in the engine. Visible and accessible to human observers.

nodes.title: Grassland Decline -- Conservation Concern
nodes.description: Native grasslands heavily converted. Ground nesting vulnerable to agriculture. Continental population decline. State species of concern in Washington. Habitat loss is the primary threat.

nodes.title: Long-eared/Short-eared Pair Complete (139-140)
nodes.description: Same genus (Asio), opposite everything: nocturnal/diurnal, forest/grassland, secretive/visible, prominent tufts/tiny tufts. The most complete comparison pair in the engine. Only shared trait: communal winter roosts.

nodes.title: Sixth Consecutive Strigidae (135-140)
nodes.description: Six consecutive owl entries. Three genera (Bubo, Strix, Asio). Four species. Two double entries. One comparison pair. Energy: 4, 5, 4, 6, 4, 5. The deepest family documentation in the engine.

cross_references.relationship: ASIO COMPARISON PAIR! Long-eared (139, nocturnal, forest, secretive, E=4) vs. Short-eared (140, diurnal, grassland, visible, E=5). Same genus, opposite habits. The most complete comparison pair in the engine.
STRIGIDAE SIX! GHO (135-136), Barred (137-138), Long-eared (139), Short-eared (140). Six consecutive. Three genera. The owl era at full depth.
STRIGIDAE FAMILY! Seven entries total. The most represented family. Spotted Owl (4), GHO (135-136), Barred (137-138), Long-eared (139), Short-eared (140).
DISPLAY COMPARISON! GHO pair duet (136, E=5, vocal display) vs. Short-eared wing-clap (140, E=5, vocal + mechanical display). Both E=5 displays. The Short-eared adds the mechanical dimension.
